items.title: Spam filter with wrong confidence
items.scenario: A binary spam classifier outputs q(spam)=0.05 for an email that is actually spam (p(spam)=1). The per-example cross-entropy is −log(0.05) ≈ 3.0 nats — a large penalty for assigning low probability to the true class.
items.explanation: Cross-entropy grows sharply as q(y_true) → 0, which is why miscalibrated but confident wrong predictions hurt training and evaluation.

items.title: Three-way image classifier
items.scenario: True label: dog (class 2). Model softmax gives π = [0.1, 0.2, 0.7]. Cross-entropy loss = −log(0.7) ≈ 0.36 nats. If the model had output π = [0.6, 0.3, 0.1], loss = −log(0.1) ≈ 2.3 nats.
items.explanation: Only the probability on the true class enters the loss; improving π_y directly lowers H(P, Q) for that one-hot P.

items.title: Language model next-token prediction
items.scenario: A tokenizer vocabulary of 50,000 tokens. The model outputs a distribution Q over tokens; the true next token is 'Paris'. Training minimizes −log q('Paris'), averaged over corpus positions — cross-entropy in bits or nats per token.
items.explanation: Perplexity exp(H) summarizes the same objective: lower cross-entropy means the model assigns more mass to what actually occurred.

items.title: Label smoothing as soft P
items.scenario: Instead of one-hot P, use p(y)=0.9 and p(other)=0.1/(K−1) spread across wrong classes. Cross-entropy becomes −0.9 log q(y) − Σ_{k≠y} (0.1/(K−1)) log q(k), discouraging overconfident softmax outputs.
items.explanation: Soft targets still fit H(P, Q); they change the effective P while Q remains the model distribution.
